section.title: The Anointing and the Last Supper
section.sectionRationale: The passion begins. As the leaders plot and Judas bargains, a woman lavishes costly perfume on Jesus for His burial. At the Passover He unmasks His betrayer and institutes the Lord's Supper, declaring the bread His body and the cup His blood of the covenant, poured out for the forgiveness of sins.
section.thesis: On the eve of the cross, extravagant devotion and cold betrayal stand side by side, while Jesus transforms the Passover into the memorial of His own death, giving His body and His covenant blood, poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the plot to kill Jesus; the anointing at Bethany; Judas's bargain; the preparation of the Passover; and the Last Supper with the betrayer unmasked and the covenant meal instituted.

verses.text: When Jesus had finished saying all these things, He told His disciples,
“You know that the Passover is two days away, and the Son of Man will be handed over to be crucified.”
At that time the chief priests and elders of the people assembled in the courtyard of the high priest, whose name was Caiaphas,
and they conspired to arrest Jesus covertly and kill Him.
“But not during the feast,” they said, “or there may be a riot among the people.”
While Jesus was in Bethany in the home of Simon the Leper,
a woman came to Him with an alabaster jar of expensive {{myron|perfume}}, which she poured on His head as He reclined at the table.
When the disciples saw this, they were indignant and asked, “Why this waste?
This perfume could have been sold at a high price, and the money given to the poor.”
Aware of this, Jesus asked, “Why are you bothering this woman? She has done a beautiful deed to Me.
The poor you will always have with you, but you will not always have Me.
By pouring this perfume on Me, she has prepared My body for burial.
Truly I tell you, wherever this gospel is preached in all the world, what she has done will also be told in memory of her.”
Then one of the Twelve, the one called Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests
and asked, “What are you willing to give me if I hand Him over to you?” And they set out for him thirty pieces of silver.
So from then on Judas looked for an opportunity to betray Jesus.
On the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Where do You want us to prepare for You to eat the {{pascha|Passover}}?”
He answered, “Go into the city to a certain man and tell him that the Teacher says, ‘My time is near. I will keep the Passover with My disciples at your house.’”
So the disciples did as Jesus had directed them and prepared the Passover.

units.label: The Last Supper
units.note: this is My body, My blood of the covenant

verses.text: When evening came, Jesus was reclining with the twelve disciples.
And while they were eating, He said to them, “Truly I tell you, one of you will betray Me.”
They were deeply grieved and began to ask Him one after another, “Surely not I, Lord?”
Jesus answered, “The one who has dipped his hand into the bowl with Me will betray Me.
The Son of Man will go just as it is written about Him, but woe to that man by whom He is betrayed. It would be better for him if he had not been born.”
Then Judas, who would betray Him, said, “Surely not I, Rabbi?” Jesus answered, “You have said it yourself.”
While they were eating, Jesus took bread, spoke a blessing and broke it, and gave it to the disciples, saying, “Take and eat; this is My body.”
Then He took the cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them, saying, “Drink from it, all of you.
This is My blood of the {{diatheke|covenant}}, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.
I tell you, I will not drink of this fruit of the vine from now on until that day when I drink it anew with you in My Father’s kingdom.”
And when they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives.

myron.gloss: Fragrant ointment or perfume, here very costly. The woman's lavish outpouring on Jesus' head is an act of anointing and, He says, a preparation of His body for burial, a beautiful deed that the gospel will forever remember.

pascha.gloss: Passover, the feast commemorating Israel's redemption from Egypt through the blood of the lamb. Jesus keeps this Passover and fills it with new meaning, for He is the true Lamb whose blood delivers His people from sin and death.

diatheke.gloss: Covenant, a binding arrangement God establishes. Jesus calls the cup His blood of the covenant, echoing Sinai and the new covenant of Jeremiah 31; His death seals the promise of forgiveness and a renewed relationship with God for many.

basic.context: Jesus tells His disciples that in two days, at Passover, He will be crucified. As the leaders plot His death, a woman anoints Him at Bethany with costly perfume; the disciples call it waste, but Jesus calls it a beautiful deed preparing Him for burial. Judas agrees to betray Him for thirty pieces of silver. At the Passover meal Jesus announces that one of them will betray Him, then takes bread and cup and gives new meaning to them: this is My body, this is My blood of the covenant, poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.
basic.hermeneutics: Matthew frames the meal between devotion and betrayal, the woman and Judas, to show what draws near to Jesus and what turns away. The Supper transforms the Passover: Jesus is the true Passover Lamb, and His death establishes a new covenant. Read this is My body in the context of the covenant meal He is instituting, the memorial and means of grace for His church.
basic.originalLanguages: The woman pours out precious myron, perfume, for His burial. Jesus keeps the pascha, the Passover, and fills it with new meaning. And He calls the cup His blood of the diatheke, the covenant, poured out for the forgiveness of sins.
basic.typology: The Passover lamb, whose blood spared Israel, is fulfilled in Christ, the Lamb of God. The covenant blood sprinkled at Sinai and the new covenant promised by Jeremiah are fulfilled in Jesus' blood of the covenant.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true Passover Lamb, willingly going to the cross as the Scriptures foretold. He is the covenant maker whose blood, poured out for many, secures the forgiveness of sins and looks ahead to the feast in the Father's kingdom.
basic.god: God's saving plan unfolds even through betrayal; the Son of Man goes as it is written. God establishes His covenant and grants forgiveness through the blood of His Son, and He will bring His people to the joy of His kingdom.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12 gives the Passover lamb. Exodus 24 gives the blood of the covenant at Sinai. Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant with forgiveness of sins. Zechariah 11 lies behind the thirty pieces of silver.
basic.oneStory: The passion opens with a woman's costly love and a disciple's cold price on Jesus' head. At the Passover, Jesus reveals the meaning of the death now upon Him: He is the true Lamb, and His blood seals a new covenant of forgiveness for many. The exodus story finds its fulfillment as the greater deliverance begins, and the Supper He gives will carry His death to His people until He drinks with them anew in the kingdom.

context.literary: Matthew's passion narrative begins here with the fifth and final finished when Jesus had finished saying all these things. The anointing is sandwiched between the plot and Judas's bargain, contrasting extravagant devotion with betrayal. The Supper, with its covenant words, is the theological center of the passion, interpreting in advance the death that the rest of the narrative recounts.
context.historical: Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread commemorated the exodus; a lamb was slain and eaten with unleavened bread and cups of wine. Anointing a guest's head was an act of honor; nard or similar perfume in an alabaster jar could be worth a year's wages. Thirty pieces of silver was the price of a slave, Exodus 21:32. Reclining at table and singing the Hallel psalms were customary at the Passover meal.
context.canonical: The Supper draws Exodus 12 (the Passover lamb), Exodus 24:8 (the blood of the covenant), and Jeremiah 31:31-34 (the new covenant with forgiveness) into one. Thirty pieces of silver echoes Zechariah 11:12-13. Paul hands on the same words in 1 Corinthians 11:23-26, and 1 Corinthians 5:7 names Christ our Passover lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To interpret Jesus' imminent death as the true Passover and the establishment of a new covenant, contrasting wholehearted devotion with betrayal, and to give His church the memorial meal of His body and blood, poured out for the forgiveness of sins.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the plot, the anointing, and the institution of the Supper. It prescribes, by Jesus' own repetition, the ongoing observance of the Supper, and it commends the woman's lavish devotion as a pattern, while warning by Judas's example against betraying Christ for gain.

debates.question: In what sense is the bread Jesus' body and the cup His blood?

views.label: A memorial and spiritual communion
views.case: Jesus was bodily present holding the bread, so this is My body is understood figuratively; the Supper is a memorial that proclaims His death and a means by which believers spiritually feed on Christ by faith, as He interprets the cup as the covenant, not literal blood.

views.label: A real, covenantal partaking of Christ
views.case: The words are weighty and the meal is a true participation in Christ's body and blood, 1 Corinthians 10:16; however the presence is understood, the Supper genuinely conveys the benefits of His sacrifice, not a bare remembrance.

debates.bearing: All orthodox readings agree the Supper proclaims and applies Christ's atoning death, uniting believers to Him and to one another; the differences concern the mode of His presence, not the reality that His body and blood, given for us, are the meal's true content.

study.originalLanguages: The woman breaks open her alabaster jar and pours out precious myron, perfume worth a fortune, on Jesus' head; what the disciples call waste He calls a beautiful deed, a preparation of His body for burial. He keeps the pascha, the Passover, Israel's feast of redemption, and at its table fills the ancient signs with new meaning: the broken bread is His body, and the cup is His blood of the diatheke, the covenant, poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins. The exodus deliverance through the lamb's blood is fulfilled as the true Lamb prepares to be slain, sealing a new covenant that grants the forgiveness the old could only foreshadow.

typology.fulfillment: Christ our Passover, whose blood spares His people from judgment and death, Exodus 12, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

typology.type: The blood of the covenant at Sinai
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' blood of the covenant, ratifying the new covenant relationship with God, Exodus 24:8, Hebrews 9:11-22.
The price of betrayal foretold in Zechariah, the shepherd valued at a slave's price, Zechariah 11:12-13.

study.christ: Jesus is the true Passover Lamb, going knowingly and willingly to the cross exactly as the Scriptures foretell, even as devotion and betrayal swirl around Him. At the table He interprets His own death: the bread broken is His body given, and the cup is His blood of the covenant, poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins. He is both the maker and the mediator of the new covenant, sealing in His own blood the promise of full forgiveness and a restored relationship with God. And He looks past the cross to the feast in the Father's kingdom, where He will drink with His people anew, so that the Supper is at once a memorial of His death and a pledge of the coming joy.
study.god: God's redemptive purpose moves forward even through human plotting and betrayal; the Son of Man goes just as it is written, so that the cross is no accident but the outworking of God's saving plan. God is the covenant-making God who at Sinai bound Himself to His people in blood and who now, through the blood of His Son, establishes the new covenant Jeremiah promised, with sins forgiven and hearts renewed. He grants forgiveness to many at the cost of His Son's poured-out life, and He purposes to bring His people at last to the feast of His kingdom, where the cup will be drunk anew in unbroken joy.

crossReferences.note: Moses sprinkles the blood of the covenant, the pattern for Jesus' words over the cup.
The new covenant with the forgiveness of sins, fulfilled in Jesus' blood.
Thirty pieces of silver, the price of the rejected shepherd, matching Judas's bargain.
Paul's transmission of the Supper, proclaiming the Lord's death until He comes.

study.oneStory: The passion opens with costly love poured on Jesus' head and a disciple's cold price set on it. At the Passover, Jesus unveils the meaning of the death now upon Him: He is the true Lamb, and His blood seals a new covenant of forgiveness for many. The exodus finds its fulfillment as the greater deliverance begins, and the meal He institutes will carry His atoning death to His church down the ages, until He drinks the cup anew with His people in the Father's kingdom. From here the narrative moves to Gethsemane and the cross.

quiz.q: How did Jesus interpret the woman's anointing at Bethany?

options.text: As a beautiful deed that prepared His body for burial, to be remembered wherever the gospel is preached.
options.note: Verses 10-13 show Jesus honoring her act as burial preparation.

options.text: As a kind gesture that showed her love for Him.
options.note: It was loving, but Jesus gave it deeper meaning, preparation for His burial, which this omits.

options.text: As a wasteful act that should have benefited the poor.
options.note: That was the disciples' complaint, which Jesus rejected.

options.text: As an attempt to make Him a king.
options.note: Jesus tied it to His burial, not to political kingship.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say the cup represented at the Last Supper?

options.text: His blood of the covenant, poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.
options.note: Verse 28 gives the covenant and forgiveness meaning of the cup.

options.text: A memorial of the exodus from Egypt.
options.note: The Passover recalls the exodus, but Jesus fills the cup with new meaning, His covenant blood, which this misses.

options.text: A symbol of the coming political kingdom.
options.note: He speaks of the Father's kingdom, but the cup's meaning is His blood poured out for forgiveness.

options.text: Simply an ordinary cup of wine with no meaning.
options.note: Jesus gives it profound covenantal significance.

quiz.q: How does Matthew present Jesus' death in this passage?

options.text: As the foretold, purposeful fulfillment of Scripture: the Son of Man goes as it is written.
options.note: Verses 2 and 24 present the cross as God's foretold plan, not mere tragedy.

options.text: As an unfortunate result of the leaders' plotting.
options.note: The plot is real, but Jesus foretells and embraces the cross as Scripture's fulfillment, which this reduces.

options.text: As something that took Jesus by surprise.
options.note: Jesus announces it in advance, showing no surprise.

options.text: As a defeat that ended His mission.
options.note: He presents it as the means of forgiveness and covenant, not defeat.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Judas's thirty pieces of silver?

options.text: It was the price of betrayal, echoing the rejected shepherd valued in Zechariah's prophecy.
options.note: The sum fulfills Zechariah 11:12-13, the price of a slave for the shepherd.

options.text: It was simply the payment Judas negotiated for his help.
options.note: It was a payment, but its prophetic weight as the price of betrayal is the deeper point.

options.text: It was a large fortune that made Judas wealthy.
options.note: Thirty pieces of silver was the price of a slave, not a great fortune.

options.text: It had no particular meaning in the story.
options.note: Matthew presents it as fulfilling Scripture, so it carries real significance.

questions: The woman gave something extravagant and was criticized for it. Where might wholehearted devotion to Jesus look like waste to others, and are you willing to offer it anyway?
Judas bargained Jesus away for silver. What smaller price are you sometimes tempted to accept in exchange for faithfulness to Christ?
At the table Jesus said the cup is His blood poured out for the forgiveness of your sins. How does dwelling on that change the way you come to the Lord's Supper?
Devotion and betrayal sat at the same table. When you examine your own heart, which way is it leaning toward Jesus right now?
Jesus looked past the cross to drinking anew in the Father's kingdom. How does the promise of that future feast strengthen you to follow Him through hard days?

translationNotes.note: On the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread is literally on the first of the Unleavened; the meal Jesus prepares is the Passover, which He fills with new covenant meaning.
Some manuscripts read the new covenant; the blood of the covenant echoes Exodus 24:8, and poured out for many recalls the servant of Isaiah 53:12 who bore the sin of many.
The fruit of the vine and the promise to drink it anew in the Father's kingdom point beyond the cross to the messianic feast, the marriage supper of the Lamb.
